044.subtitle: A grand scale synchronized fireworks and music show, the earliest beach fireworks festival in Japan held in April.
paragraphs: This annual event takes place in mid-April at Ginowan Seaside Park. The highlight is the intensive launch of over 10,000 fireworks within an hour, marking the beginning of summer in Japan.

The festival features a precise synchronization of fireworks and music. With the launch site located along the coastline, the beach viewing area offers an unobstructed view, and many food stalls providing local Okinawan cuisine and traditional Japanese snacks are set up on-site.

For transportation, visitors typically depart from Naha city. They can choose to take the festival shuttle bus from the bus terminal next to Asahibashi Station or use city buses. The organizers also sell special paid viewing tickets that include shuttle service each year.

045.subtitle: A daily display of fireworks over half a year, launched along the scenic hot spring town.
paragraphs: This is a rare long-term continuous event in Japan, held annually from April 28 to October 31. Throughout the rainy season and summer, fireworks are launched promptly at 20:45 for about 20 minutes by the shores of Lake Toya.

The launch method is quite unique, with fireworks being fired from a boat while it navigates across the lake. This design allows guests in lakeside hotel rooms along the several-kilometer stretch of Lake Toya Onsen to enjoy a direct view from their windows.

There is no need to purchase tickets for this event; travelers can simply stay at one of the inns along the hot spring street or walk to the lakeside promenade to watch for free. Since the schedule spans multiple seasons, it has become a staple for summer and autumn tourism in Hokkaido.

046.subtitle: The grand finale of the Yokohama Port Festival series, showcasing summer fireworks against the backdrop of the Minato Mirai 21 waterfront night view.
paragraphs: This annual celebration is held to commemorate the opening of Yokohama Port, taking place on June 1st and 2nd at the main venue in Inland Park, Minato Mirai 21. The fireworks display serves as the highlight of the entire festival.

Typically featuring between 3,000 to 5,000 fireworks, the show is synchronized with modern music and high-tech laser light displays over the coastal bay. In June, when fireworks are generally scarce across Japan due to the rainy season, this event stands out as the most significant in the Kanto region.

Access is extremely convenient, with direct connections from central Tokyo via the Tokyu Toyoko Line to the Minato Mirai Line, allowing visitors to easily reach the venue by getting off at either 'Minato Mirai Station' or 'Bashamichi Station'. This makes it a highly accessible urban event for travelers in June.

047.subtitle: A ticketed music art fireworks show at the Kyoto Racecourse, featuring a spectacular scale of up to 10,000 shots.
paragraphs: This top-tier fireworks display is held on the first Wednesday of June at the JRA Kyoto Racecourse. The event operates on a fully reserved seating system, with fixed seats, well-equipped restrooms, and convenient dining options, eliminating the need to scramble for spots on the streets.

With a scale of up to 13,000 shots, it is crafted by Japan's top fireworks teams. The highlight is the use of computer ignition technology that synchronizes with classic melodies to create precise explosions every 1/30th of a second, making it the highest standard and most complete regular fireworks festival in Japan for the month of June.

The venue is easily accessible, located in the Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City. Travelers can take the Keihan Electric Railway to Yodo Station, and after exiting, they can walk directly to the racecourse venue via a connecting pedestrian bridge in about 2 minutes.

048.subtitle: A one-hour display of 15,000 fireworks, the earliest summer festival along the Arakawa River in Tokyo.
paragraphs: This is the earliest large-scale summer fireworks festival in Tokyo, held in late July each year, located along the Arakawa Riverbank (on both the Senju and Kosuge sides). Its standout feature is the high-density launch rhythm, with all 15,000 fireworks released within just one hour.

The program includes traditional fireworks, Western-style fireworks, and a grand finale featuring the stunning golden weeping willow fireworks lit horizontally. As the opening event of Tokyo's summer fireworks season, the riverbanks attract large crowds dressed in yukatas and various traditional festival stalls.

While access is convenient, the area can get quite crowded, with the main stations being Kitasenju, Kosuge, and Gotanno. Due to the spacious venue along the Arakawa riverbank, many visitors who do not purchase paid seats arrive early to secure spots on the grassy areas for free viewing.

049.subtitle: Experience theater-quality sound effects created by the mountainous coastal basin, with fireworks displayed throughout the year.
paragraphs: The Atami Fireworks Festival has been held since 1952 as a traditional event that takes place seasonally and irregularly throughout the year, with the summer shows in July and August being the most frequent. The fireworks are launched from a fixed location in Atami Bay, spanning from Atami Sun Beach to the nearby waterfront park.

Each show lasts about 20 to 25 minutes. Due to Atami's natural basin topography, surrounded by mountains on three sides and facing the sea, the sound of the fireworks creates a strong echo against the mountain walls, resulting in a deep bass vibration similar to that of a sports arena.

For transportation, take the Shinkansen or JR Tōkaidō Main Line to 'Atami Station' and walk about 15 to 20 minutes to reach the beach viewing area. Many weekends in the summer coincide with national holidays, attracting large crowds of tourists and visitors staying at hot spring inns.

001.subtitle: A flagship festival with a century-old tradition, renowned throughout Japan, featuring simultaneous displays at two venues.
paragraphs: This festival is Japan's oldest fireworks event, originating from the Edo period in 1733 (the 18th year of the Kyoho era) during the 'Sumida River Opening.' It is held annually on the last Saturday of July, with displays taking place from Sakurabashi to Kototoibashi (Venue 1) and from Komagata Bridge to Umayabashi (Venue 2).

The total number of fireworks launched from both venues reaches an impressive 20,000. The sight of fireworks soaring into the sky next to the Tokyo Skytree is a signature highlight, attracting over 900,000 to a million domestic and international visitors to the streets and parks along the Sumida River each year.

Due to its location in the heart of Tokyo, there will be extremely strict one-way pedestrian controls around Asakusa Station, Oshiage Station, and Ryogoku Station on the night of the event. Most ground-level streets are obscured by buildings, so independent travelers should arrive early to secure spots in designated parks.

002.subtitle: The grand finale of Japan's three major festivals, where bonfire boats and plum blossom fireworks blend in a religious celebration.
paragraphs: This is the main event of the Osaka Tenjin Matsuri, held annually on the evening of July 25. The fireworks are launched along the banks of the Okawa River, at the border of the Miyakojima and Kita wards (Kawasaki Park and Otemae Sakuranomiya Park).

Approximately 3,000 to 5,000 fireworks are launched, including the special 'Plum Blossom Fireworks' shaped like plum blossoms, exclusive to Tenmangu Shrine. The most iconic sight is the simultaneous display of fireworks in the sky and the bonfire boats of the 'Funato Gyo' fleet, which features over a hundred boats lit with bonfires on the river surface.

The main access routes are via the Osaka Metro Tanimachi Line at 'Tenmabashi Station' or JR Tozai Line at 'Sakuranomiya Station'. On the day of the event, strict crowd control measures will be implemented at all bridges and food stall areas, aside from ticketed viewing areas along the riverbanks.

003.subtitle: A prestigious competition featuring up to 20,000 fireworks launched over a 2-kilometer wide field.
paragraphs: This festival is one of the highest-rated fireworks competitions in Japan, held annually in late July or early August at Aino Park in Bagoi City, Shizuoka Prefecture, with a total of nearly 20,000 fireworks launched.

Set in a spacious suburban area, the fireworks launch site stretches horizontally for 2 kilometers. The event features high-difficulty rapid-fire shells synchronized with music, as well as special fountain fireworks and giant suspended waterfalls designed to resemble Mount Fuji.

Access primarily relies on the JR Tōkaidō Line. Travelers need to take a train to either Aino Station or Bagoi Station, and then follow the crowd for about a 20-minute walk to reach the venue. As a professional competition, it attracts photographers and fireworks enthusiasts from all over Japan.

004.subtitle: A historic tribute to the fallen and a beacon of local revival, featuring a traditional riverside display of ten thousand fireworks.
paragraphs: This traditional festival has been held since 1953 (Showa 28) to honor war casualties and pray for local disaster recovery. It is typically held in late July at the Abe River Aquatic Park (Yanagicho Riverside) in Shizuoka City.

The display usually features around ten thousand fireworks, including large shell fireworks and rapid-fire pyrotechnics. Organized by local government and business groups, the spacious riverbank allows spectators to experience the sound of the explosions up close.

For transportation, visitors can disembark at JR Shizuoka Station. On the day of the festival, a paid official shuttle bus is usually available in front of the station, taking about 10 to 15 minutes. If walking, it takes approximately 35 minutes to reach the riverside venue.

005.subtitle: The grand finale of Toyota City's annual festival, featuring a spectacular musical fireworks display crafted by Japan's top pyrotechnicians.
paragraphs: This is the grand finale show of the 'Toyota OIDEN Festival' held on the last Sunday of July in Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture, located at Shirahama Park along the Yahagi River. With a total of 13,000 fireworks launched, it is one of the most iconic large-scale fireworks festivals in the Tokai region.

The event is executed by a top team of pyrotechnicians who have won the Prime Minister's Award. It features precision aerial fireworks synchronized with music, as well as giant suspended fireworks known as 'Niagara Falls,' extending 500 meters horizontally and reaching heights of several dozen meters.

Access is straightforward. Visitors can take the Meitetsu Toyota Line or Mikawa Line to 'Toyoda City Station,' or take the Aichi Loop Railway to 'Shin-Toyota Station,' and then walk about 15 to 20 minutes to reach the riverside venue. There will be extensive vehicle restrictions in the surrounding area that evening.

006.subtitle: Famous for its expansive ocean displays, featuring the giant 'San-shaku-dama' and rapid-fire bursts, part of the three great fireworks of Echigo.
paragraphs: This traditional event, ranked among the 'Three Great Fireworks of Echigo', is held annually on July 26 at Minato Town Seaside Park (Central Coast) in Kashiwazaki City, Niigata Prefecture. The festival unfolds over the vast surface of the Sea of Japan, showcasing approximately 15,000 fireworks.

The main highlights include the rare 'San-shaku-dama' super-sized fireworks launched from the sea, as well as the 'Waterborne Rapid-Fire Fireworks' that burst in a fan shape over the water. The grand finale features a stunning display of 100 giant shells fired continuously over six minutes, providing a breathtaking visual experience.

Access is primarily via the JR Shin'etsu Main Line or the Echigo Line. Travelers can take the train to 'Kashiwazaki Station' and walk about 20 minutes towards the coast to reach the beach viewing area. Due to the seaside location, there is a postponement mechanism in case of strong winds or heavy rain.

007.subtitle: Sapporo's only summer fireworks event in the city center, a half-century tradition held by the Toyohira River.
paragraphs: This is a staple summer event in Sapporo, held on the last Friday of July (postponed in case of rain), co-hosted by the Hokkaido Shimbun and Hokkaido Cultural Broadcasting (UHB). The venue is located along the Toyohira River in downtown Sapporo (between Minami-Ohashi and Horohira Bridge).

Approximately 4,000 fireworks are launched, and the event is divided into three phases. It features various creative fireworks, large aerial displays, and rapid-fire fireworks synchronized with popular music. This is the most accessible urban fireworks festival for travelers visiting Hokkaido to escape the summer heat.

The location is excellent. Travelers can take the Sapporo City Subway Namboku Line to 'Nakajima Park Station' or 'Horohira Bridge Station', and it takes only a 5-minute walk from the station to reach the riverside grassy area. Due to the short distance, the urban viewing area becomes extremely crowded that evening.

008.subtitle: A metropolitan-style musical fireworks display with up to 20,000 shots at Chiba's Makuhari Seaside.
paragraphs: This is the largest beach fireworks show in Chiba Prefecture, held on the first Saturday of August (or the last Saturday of July) at the beach of Makuhari Seaside Park in Mihama Ward, Chiba City. A total of 20,000 fireworks are launched, showcasing a modern and high-performance urban experience.

Highlights of the event include ultra-large rapid-fire fireworks perfectly synchronized with modern music, giant water fireworks exploding over Tokyo Bay, and special performances where audience members interact with glowing props. There are also extensive paid viewing areas with large seating and picnic mat options.

Traffic flow is smooth. Visitors can take the JR Keiyo Line to Kaihin-Makuhari Station and walk about 15 minutes to reach the beach venue. With the Makuhari Messe exhibition center and several large shopping malls nearby, dining and essential facilities are very well-equipped.

009.subtitle: A river fireworks display renowned for its opening thunder with a thousand fireworks launched in five seconds, co-hosted with Ichikawa City.
paragraphs: This is one of Tokyo's most iconic large-scale fireworks festivals, held on the first Saturday of August. Co-hosted by Edogawa Ward in Tokyo and Ichikawa City in Chiba Prefecture across the Edogawa River, the total number of spectators on both sides often exceeds one million.

A total of 14,000 fireworks are launched, divided into eight different music-themed segments. The most famous feature is the 'Opening Show,' where 1,000 golden fireworks are launched in just five seconds from multiple launch points, instantly elevating the atmosphere to its peak.

Access varies depending on the viewing side. The Edogawa side mainly utilizes the Toei Shinjuku Line's 'Shinozaki Station,' a 15-minute walk, or the JR Sobu Line's 'Koiwa Station,' a 25-minute walk; while the Ichikawa side uses JR 'Ichikawa Station.' There will be a large-scale traffic ban in the surrounding area that evening.

050.subtitle: A spectacular event on the Arakawa River, featuring Kanto's longest waterfall and giant shell fireworks, competing with the city of Toda across the river.
paragraphs: This large-scale fireworks display in Tokyo is held on the first Saturday of August, located at the Arakawa Riverbank in Itabashi. It simultaneously launches fireworks from both sides of the river, alongside the 'Toda Bridge Fireworks Festival' in Saitama Prefecture, with a total of up to 15,000 fireworks launched.

The festival is renowned for its 'large-sized fireworks.' Attendees can witness the rare '15-inch shells' (giant fireworks with a diameter of 400 meters) that are seldom seen in the Tokyo metropolitan area, as well as the 'Niagara Falls' ground-mounted fire fountain, which is known to be the longest in the Kanto region at a total length of 700 meters.

Access is primarily via the Toei Mita Line. Travelers can disembark at 'Takashimadaira Station,' 'Nishidai Station,' or 'Hasune Station' and walk approximately 20 to 30 minutes to the riverbank. The expansive grassy area offers a wide-open view, making it a popular spot for locals in Tokyo.

010.subtitle: A historical festival celebrating the birthplace of Tokugawa Ieyasu, featuring traditional Mikawa fireworks.
paragraphs: This is the most historically significant fireworks festival in Aichi Prefecture, held on the first Saturday of August. The launch site is located at the confluence of the Yoshikawa and Yahagigawa rivers, right next to the famous Okazaki Castle keep. The number of fireworks launched is in the tens of thousands.

The core of the event is the display of the local 'Mikawa fireworks' traditional craft. In addition to large aerial fireworks, the most distinctive feature is the handmade 'hand-held fireworks' (where a firework artisan holds a bamboo tube and shoots fire into the sky), along with specially designed pattern fireworks that rise against the backdrop of Okazaki Castle.

Transportation can be taken via the Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line to 'Higashi-Okazaki Station' or 'Okazaki Koen-mae Station', with a 10 to 15-minute walk to the riverbanks. The view of the castle framed by the fireworks is steeped in historical significance, making it a hotspot for photography enthusiasts in Japan.

011.subtitle: The most representative waterfront fireworks display in the Hokuriku region, illuminating the banks of the Okawa River every summer.
paragraphs: This is one of the largest traditional fireworks festivals in the Hokuriku region (Ishikawa, Toyama, Fukui), held on the first Saturday of August each year (postponed in case of bad weather) at the green parks around Okawa in Kanazawa City (along the Tedorigawa or designated waterfront areas).

With a total of 10,000 fireworks launched, the program is tightly packed, featuring rapid-fire fireworks, large-scale aerial displays, and special water fireworks that reflect dazzling colors on the river's surface, making it a central attraction for summer tourism in Ishikawa Prefecture.

On the transportation front, the organizers typically provide temporary shuttle services from JR Kanazawa Station or other major railway hubs on the day of the event. It is recommended that travelers without reserved seats arrive early in the evening to secure a good viewing spot.

012.subtitle: A century-old tradition dating back to the Meiji era, featuring 20,000 dazzling fireworks along the Watarase River.
paragraphs: Founded in 1903 (Meiji 36), this festival boasts a prestigious history of over a century. It is held on the first Saturday of August at the riverside below Tanaka Bridge on the Watarase River in Ashikaga City, Tochigi Prefecture, showcasing an impressive scale of 20,000 fireworks, making it one of the most famous events in northern Kanto.

The festival features creative competitive fireworks crafted by master pyrotechnicians from across Japan, large continuous rapid-fire displays, and a breathtaking 'aerial waterfall' that stretches hundreds of meters, showering golden sparks. On the night of the event, hundreds of traditional Japanese festival stalls line both sides of the riverbank.

Visitors can take the JR Ryomo Line to 'Ashikaga Station' and reach the riverside venue in just 5 minutes on foot; alternatively, they can take the Tobu Isesaki Line to 'Ashikagashi Station' and walk about 10 minutes, making it very accessible for travelers.

013.subtitle: A stunning festival held in the expansive Watarase Wetlands, renowned for its massive three-foot fireworks.
paragraphs: This is the annual flagship fireworks display in Koga City, Ibaraki Prefecture, held on the first Saturday of August. The launch site is located at the nationally designated wetland 'Watarase Wetlands' (riverbank next to Koga Golf Course), with a total of up to 20,000 fireworks launched.

The festival's main highlight is the launch of the 'three-foot' giant fireworks. Weighing over 200 kilograms, these fireworks explode at a diameter of up to 650 meters. With no tall buildings obstructing the view around the wetlands, the visual and sound waves from the explosions are considered the best in the Kanto region.

Transportation is available via the JR East Utsunomiya Line or the Ueno-Tokyo Line, with direct access to 'Koga Station'. After exiting the station, follow the signs for about a 20-minute walk to reach the viewing area along the embankment. Due to the vast open space, the free viewing areas also offer excellent perspectives.

014.subtitle: One of Japan's premier lakeside fireworks displays, showcasing rapid-fire bursts on the surface of Lake Shinji.
paragraphs: This is the most representative large-scale fireworks festival in the San'in region, held on the first weekend of August. The launch site is located on the eastern surface of Lake Shinji in Matsue City, Shimane Prefecture, with a total of 21,000 fireworks launched over the two days.

The festival makes full use of the expansive surface of Lake Shinji, featuring 'waterborne rapid-fire fireworks' that explode in a semi-circular pattern on the lake, as well as multiple large-scale aerial fireworks that ignite in succession. The colorful lights reflecting on the lake's surface are its core iconic scenery.

Visitors can take the JR San'in Main Line to 'Matsue Station' and walk about 15 minutes to reach the lakeside viewing area; alternatively, they can use the Ichibata Electric Railway to 'Matsue Shinji Lake Onsen Station.' On the night of the event, there will be extensive vehicle restrictions on surrounding roads.

016.subtitle: A flagship spectacle that evolved from a World War II memorial, featuring the stunning Shōsan Shaku-dama and Phoenix fireworks that captivate all of Japan.
paragraphs: Founded in 1946, it initially served as a memorial event to honor the citizens who lost their lives during World War II. It is held annually on August 2 and 3 at the Shinano River Riverside (upstream of Chōsei Bridge) in Nagaoka City, Niigata Prefecture, with a total of 20,000 fireworks launched over the two days.

The main attraction is the so-called 'Shōsan Shaku-dama,' which is one of the largest in Japan, measuring 90 cm in diameter and weighing up to 300 kg, along with the 'Phoenix Fireworks' that create a display spanning 2 kilometers. The finale features the 'Shirogiku Fireworks,' a large white firework that pays tribute to the souls of the departed.

Travelers can take the JR Joetsu Shinkansen to 'Nagaoka Station' and walk about 20 minutes from the station. Due to the high attendance of 700,000 to 1 million people over the two days, Shinkansen tickets must be purchased immediately on the public sale date, and the wait time for return trains that evening typically exceeds one hour.

018.subtitle: A 350-year tradition honoring the Kurume Suitengu Shrine, featuring top-tier traditional fireworks in two venues
paragraphs: Founded in 1650 (the 3rd year of the Keian era), this traditional offering event has continued to celebrate the completion of the Kurume Suitengu Shrine. It is held annually on August 5th at the riverside of the Chikugo River in Kurume City, Fukuoka Prefecture, with a staggering total of 15,000 fireworks launched.

The event sets up multiple launch points along the Chikugo River, divided into several viewing areas such as the 'Komorino Venue' and the 'Nagamotoishi Venue,' where fireworks are launched simultaneously. The program includes large aerial fireworks and rapid-fire displays, making it one of the largest summer events in Kitakyushu.

Transportation is available via the JR Kagoshima Main Line to 'Kurume Station,' from which it is about a 10-minute walk to the nearest riverside venue; alternatively, you can take the Nishitetsu Tenjin Omuta Line to 'Nishitetsu Kurume Station' and transfer to the festival's shuttle bus. The expansive area offers excellent views from the grassy zones.

019.subtitle: As the pre-festival event of the Sendai Tanabata Festival, one of Japan's three major Tanabata festivals, it lights up the urban riverside with fireworks.
paragraphs: This large-scale fireworks display is held annually on August 5th as a prelude to the official Sendai Tanabata Festival the following day. The launch site is located along the Hirose River in Aoba Ward, Sendai City, with approximately 16,000 fireworks being set off.

The event features high-density rapid-fire fireworks and various creative pattern fireworks in urban green spaces. Being close to Sendai's bustling city center, the entire city is filled with a festive atmosphere that evening, with streets adorned with large Tanabata bamboo decorations and food stalls.

Visitors can take the Sendai City Subway Tozai Line to either Omachi Nishi Park Station or Kokusai Center Station, and reach the viewing area upon exiting; alternatively, it is about a 25-minute walk from JR Sendai Station. Major roads in the city center will be closed to traffic that evening, allowing for pedestrian access.

020.subtitle: A Kansai classic showcasing spectacular wide-angle aerial fireworks over Japan's largest lake, Lake Biwa.
paragraphs: This is Shiga Prefecture's most representative annual event, typically held in early August. The launch site is located offshore at Otsu Port on Lake Biwa, with a total of up to 10,000 fireworks being launched.

The festival's main attraction is the use of multiple launch boats to perform extreme angled 'diagonal aerial fireworks' on both sides of the lake. The fireworks burst over the water, creating a stunning three-dimensional light network with the large aerial shells, resulting in a visually striking display.

Visitors can take the JR Tōkaidō Main Line (Biwako Line) to 'Otsu Station' or 'Zeze Station' and walk about 15 minutes; alternatively, they can take the Keihan Ishiyama Sakamoto Line to 'Biwako Hama-Otsu Station.' On the night of the event, crowd control around the seaside park is very strict.

021.subtitle: An epic spectacle where giant lantern boats and high-altitude fireworks unite at Aomori Port, capping off the Aomori Nebuta Festival.
paragraphs: This fireworks display is held every year on the evening of August 7, serving as the grand finale of the Aomori Nebuta Festival. The launch site is located on the inland sea at Aomori Port (around Aoi Umi Park and Shinmachi Pier), featuring approximately 11,000 fireworks.

This is the only event in Japan where you can see 'Nebuta lanterns and fireworks together.' That evening, the award-winning giant Nebuta lantern will be placed on boats for a 'maritime procession,' while thousands of high-altitude fireworks will light up the sky, creating a classic summer scene in Tohoku, Japan.

Travelers can take the JR Ou Main Line or the Tohoku Shinkansen to 'Aomori Station,' and it’s just a 5 to 10-minute walk from the station to the port area for viewing along the coast. Due to the overlap with the last day of the Nebuta Festival, nearby hotels and tickets should be reserved well in advance.

022.subtitle: A traditional spectacle co-hosted by the All Japan Chunichi Shimbun, set against the backdrop of Gifu's Kinkazan and the Nagara River.
paragraphs: This is a historic national fireworks festival in the Tokai region, held on the first Saturday of August each year (with slight adjustments in some years). The launch site is located along the riverbank of the Nagara River in Gifu City, stretching from downstream of Nagara Bridge to upstream of Kinkazan Bridge, with over 10,000 fireworks set off.

The backdrop of the festival features the famous Kinkazan and Gifu Castle atop the mountain. The program includes a continuous display of massive rapid-fire fireworks (Star Mine), nighttime colored smoke, and specially designed fireworks that burst over the clear waters of the Nagara River. Festival stalls will line both sides of the riverbank for hundreds of meters that night.

Transportation is available via the JR Tōkaidō Main Line to 'Gifu Station' or the Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line to 'Meitetsu Gifu Station.' On the day of the festival, a highly frequent official shuttle bus service will operate from in front of the station to the venue, taking about 20 minutes.

024.subtitle: Hokkaido's largest scale, digitally synchronized fireworks with music, illuminating the summer event on the Tokachi River.
paragraphs: Founded in 1929 and organized by the Tokachi Mainichi Newspaper, this is the most representative fireworks festival in Hokkaido. It is held annually on August 13 (the start of the Bon Festival holiday) along the Tokachi River (downstream of the Tokachi Bridge) in Obihiro City, featuring up to 20,000 fireworks.

The festival is renowned for its high-tech 'fully computer-controlled ignition,' which precisely synchronizes fireworks with modern music and live lighting and sound systems. The grand finale, 'Nishikigawa (Golden Weeping Willow Fireworks),' will transform the sky above the Tokachi River into a reflective golden hue in a short time.

Travelers can take the JR Nemuro Main Line to 'Obihiro Station.' On the day of the festival, temporary paid shuttle buses will be available from the station area and the Tokachi special parking lot directly to the riverside venue, with a travel time of about 15 minutes. Due to the Bon Festival holiday, there will be a large number of domestic tourists.

025.subtitle: A breathtaking maritime spectacle where Shimonoseki in Yamaguchi and Moji in Fukuoka launch fireworks across the strait simultaneously.
paragraphs: This is the only unique fireworks festival in Japan that is 'held across the strait and jointly organized by two cities,' taking place annually on August 13. Fireworks are launched simultaneously from Shimonoseki City in Yamaguchi Prefecture (along the Karato Pier) and Moji Ward in Kitakyushu City, Fukuoka Prefecture (along the Mojiko waterfront), separated by the Kanmon Straits.

A total of 15,000 fireworks are launched from both sides. The Shimonoseki side features large 'shakudama' shells and water-based self-exploding fireworks, while the Moji side focuses on high-density rapid-fire fireworks synchronized with music. The fireworks from both sides intertwine against the backdrop of the Kanmon Straits Bridge, creating a visual experience that is truly one-of-a-kind in Japan.

On the Yamaguchi side, it is a 20-minute walk from JR Sanyo Line 'Shimonoseki Station'; on the Fukuoka side, you can reach it directly from JR Kagoshima Line 'Mojiko Station.' That evening, strict crowd control measures will be implemented at transportation hubs on both sides of the Kanmon Straits.

026.subtitle: The grand finale of Shikoku's three major festivals, featuring flagship fireworks over the Seto Inland Sea.
paragraphs: This is the largest summer celebration in Takamatsu City, Kagawa Prefecture, known as the 'Sanuki Takamatsu Festival,' which is held annually on August 13 (the date may vary slightly in some years). The fireworks are launched offshore from Takamatsu Port (around Sunport Takamatsu), with approximately 8,000 fireworks displayed.

The fireworks are launched directly over the surface of the Seto Inland Sea, showcasing a combination of large aerial fireworks and water-based special effects. With the expansive view from the Sunport Takamatsu embankment, the reflections of the fireworks on the water are striking, making it the most popular fireworks event in the Shikoku region during summer.

Visitors can take the JR Yosan Line or Kotoku Line to 'Takamatsu Station,' or take the Takamatsu-Kotohira Electric Railroad Kotohira Line to 'Takamatsu-Chikko Station.' After exiting, it is only a 3 to 5-minute walk to the viewing area along the port.

027.subtitle: A classic of the Chugoku region known for its exceptionally long single launch line, taking full advantage of the extensive river terrain.
paragraphs: This is the grand finale of the 'Fukuyama Summer Festival' held annually on August 15 in Fukuyama City, Hiroshima Prefecture, located on the riverside of the 'Ashida River' (upstream of Mizunomi Bridge). Approximately 10,000 fireworks are launched, making it a representative summer event in the Chugoku region (both San'in and San'yō).

The festival takes full advantage of the wide and straight river terrain of the Ashida River, with an ultra-long launch line that stretches 1.4 kilometers set up in the middle of the riverbed. The event features a continuous rapid-fire display of fireworks with a wide horizontal spread, allowing spectators on both riverbanks to enjoy a direct view from several kilometers away.

Transportation is available via the JR Sanyo Shinkansen or Sanyo Main Line to 'Fukuyama Station'. On the day of the event, official shuttle buses will operate from the bus terminal in front of the station directly to the Ashida River riverside venue. Due to the spacious area, the viewing spots on the grassy banks on both sides offer excellent visibility.

028.subtitle: A premier competition venue acclaimed by fireworks fans across Japan, featuring a stunning 700-meter wide display.
paragraphs: This is a nationally recognized fireworks festival ranked among Japan's top events, typically held in mid-August. The location is along the Akagawa riverbed in Tsuruoka City, Yamagata Prefecture, where up to 12,000 fireworks are launched.

The festival is highly regarded among Japanese fireworks enthusiasts. The main highlights include the artistic national design fireworks and the giant ultra-wide-angle rapid-fire fireworks that extend 700 meters horizontally, perfectly synchronized with music. The grand finale featuring golden weeping willow fireworks is particularly iconic.

Transportation is available via the JR Uetsu Main Line to 'Tsuruoka Station.' After exiting the station, it takes about 30 minutes to walk to the Akagawa riverbed venue; on the day of the festival, temporary buses will also be provided at several designated transfer points within Tsuruoka City. Due to its immense popularity, professional photographers from all over Japan arrive early to secure prime spots.

029.subtitle: A premier event featuring tens of thousands of fireworks, utilizing the mountainous lake and cliffs to create stunning echoes.
paragraphs: Founded in 1949, this is the highest-scale fireworks festival in Japan, consistently launching around 40,000 fireworks each year. It takes place annually on August 15, regardless of weather, on the surface of Suwa Lake located in central Nagano Prefecture.

The festival features a specially designed launch platform in the center of the lake, showcasing massive water fireworks with diameters of up to 500 meters, as well as a giant ground installation called the 'Niagara Falls' fire fountain, which stretches 2 kilometers. Surrounded by mountains, the sound of the fireworks creates an incredible echo throughout the valleys.

Visitors can take the JR Chuo Main Line to 'Kamisuwa Station' and walk about 8 to 10 minutes to reach Lakeside Park. On the day of the event, up to 500,000 people flock to this hot spring town, causing surrounding railways and access roads to become extremely congested.

030.subtitle: A grand event in Tango, blending the Edo-period tradition of lantern floating with dazzling fireworks over the water.
paragraphs: With a history of over 400 years, this event takes place every year on August 16th at Miyazu Bay (along the coast of Shimazaki Park) in Miyazu City, Kyoto Prefecture. Originally, this event was a religious ceremony to commemorate ancestors, where lanterns were floated on the sea during the Obon festival.

On the night of the event, around 10,000 red and white paper lanterns are released onto the sea, creating a ribbon of floating lights across Miyazu Bay. At the same time, approximately 3,000 aerial fireworks are launched above the water, creating a magical scene where the surface of the water and the sky reflect each other.

Transportation primarily utilizes the Kyoto Tango Railway's Miyafuku Line. Travelers can take the train to 'Miyazu Station' and walk about 10 minutes to reach the viewing area at Shimazaki Park. Due to its proximity to one of Japan's three scenic views, Amanohashidate, many visitors combine this event with a day trip to Amanohashidate.

031.subtitle: Fireworks launched from the natural rock walls of the World Heritage Site, creating a stunning display of three-foot shells exploding over the sea.
paragraphs: With a history of over 300 years, this festival is held annually on August 17 at Shichiri Mihama Coast in Kumano City, Mie Prefecture. The launch site is adjacent to the famous 'Oni no Shita' sea-eroded rock wall, a notable spot within the World Cultural Heritage site of 'Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range.' Approximately 10,000 fireworks are set off during the event.

The main attractions include the 'Oni no Shita Grand Display' and the 'Three-Foot Shells' that explode over the sea. The organizers place the fireworks directly on two boats moving at full speed, which then toss them into the water. The fireworks explode into massive semi-circular fireballs on the sea surface, creating a powerful resonance with the rock walls.

For transportation, visitors mainly take the JR Tōkai Kisei Main Line to 'Kumano City Station,' and it’s just a 5-minute walk to the spacious viewing area at Shichiri Mihama pebble beach. Due to its relatively remote location, express trains returning that night are often in high demand.

032.subtitle: The highest honor in Japan, established during the Meiji era, featuring exclusive daytime fireworks at a sacred venue.
paragraphs: Founded in 1910 (Meiji 43), it is the most prestigious and historically significant of Japan's 'Three Great Fireworks Festivals.' It is held on the last Saturday of August at the riverside of the Omono River in Daisen City, Akita Prefecture, with a scale of up to 18,000 fireworks.

This is one of the few competitions in Japan that features 'daytime fireworks' (using colored smoke to create geometric shapes during the day). The nighttime segment sees top fireworks artisans from across Japan competing for the Prime Minister's Award, culminating in a grand finale with music and fireworks spanning a width of 900 meters.

Travelers can take the Akita Shinkansen or JR Ou Main Line to 'Omagari Station,' and then walk about 30 minutes to the riverbank. On the day of the event, the area can be crowded with up to 700,000 to 800,000 people, and both the Shinkansen and temporary buses require strict reservations, so be sure to secure your seat in advance.

033.subtitle: An autumn art show that beautifully integrates nature, sculpture, and thousands of fireworks at Moerenuma Park's geometric landscape.
paragraphs: This large-scale ticketed art fireworks show is held annually in early September at Moerenuma Park, designed by renowned sculptor Isamu Noguchi, located in the eastern district of Sapporo. With a total of 10,000 fireworks launched, the event emphasizes the harmonious blend of natural landscapes and fireworks.

The event features a fully ticketed seating system. It aims to synchronize the fireworks with a high-quality sound system, utilizing the park's unique geometric hills and glass pyramid landscape to create a visual depth that resembles three-dimensional sculptures as the fireworks explode at various heights and angles.

Traffic control is strict. As Moerenuma Park is located on the outskirts of the city, general vehicles are completely prohibited from entering on the day of the festival. Visitors must purchase official direct shuttle bus tickets in advance, departing from either Sakaemachi Station on the subway or JR Naebo Station.

034.subtitle: A spectacular display of 20,000 fireworks across the wide Mogami River, famous for its ultra-wide-angle water launch.
paragraphs: Founded in 1929 (Showa 4) to celebrate the opening of Sakata Port, this traditional festival is held every mid-September along the banks of the Mogami River in Sakata City, Yamagata Prefecture, featuring between 12,000 and 20,000 fireworks.

The festival takes full advantage of the expansive area at the mouth of the Mogami River, with a launch line that stretches over 2 kilometers wide. Key highlights include multiple musical rapid-fire shells composed of large-sized fireworks, as well as massive water-based special effects fireworks that explode simultaneously over the wide water surface.

You can take the JR Uetsu Main Line to 'Sakata Station'. On the day of the festival, paid official shuttle buses will operate from designated temporary parking areas in front of the station and nearby, directly to the riverside venue. The flat and unobstructed terrain around the area provides a stunning view and sound experience from the free viewing zones along the riverbank.

035.subtitle: The world's largest single-shell firework, the 'Shijakudama,' reaches a diameter of 800 meters, making it a world record festival in the autumn nights of Ojiya.
paragraphs: This is the autumn奉納 festival held in Katakai Town, Ojiya City, Niigata Prefecture, taking place over two consecutive days in mid-September (specific dates vary each year; in 2026, it will be held from September 11 to 12). Approximately 10,000 fireworks are launched.

The highlight of the festival is the 'Shijakudama firework.' This firework shell, with a diameter of 120 centimeters and weighing 420 kilograms, is recognized by the Guinness World Records as the largest caliber single-shell firework. When launched, it creates a massive spherical explosion in the night sky with a diameter of about 800 meters, covering nearly half of the sky.

To access the event, take the JR Joetsu Line to 'Ojiya Station,' then transfer to a temporary bus to the Katakai Town venue (approximately 20 minutes). Due to the difficulty of booking tickets for the Shijakudama, many fireworks enthusiasts choose to stay in hotels in Ojiya City and walk or bike to the venue on the day of the event.

037.subtitle: Set against the backdrop of the World Heritage Site Himeji Castle, enjoy a summer classic of close-range maritime fireworks.
paragraphs: This is a maritime event held in the Himeji Port area (around Shikama Wharf) in Himeji City, Hyogo Prefecture, fixed for mid to late September. Approximately 5,000 to 6,000 fireworks are launched.

The main highlight of the event is that the launch site is only about 300 meters from the viewing area on the beach, allowing spectators to feel the power of the fireworks exploding on the sea surface at an extremely close distance. The program includes rapid-fire fireworks synchronized with music, special fireworks in the water, and multiple large aerial salutes.

Transportation is available via the Sanyo Electric Railway Main Line to 'Sanyo Shikama Station,' from which it is about a 25-minute walk to the port area; on the day of the festival, an official shuttle bus will also operate from the south exit of JR 'Himeji Station.' The venue is mostly flat, providing an extremely open view.

038.subtitle: Organized by local volunteers from Osaka, showcasing a spectacular display of rapid-fire fireworks along the Yodogawa River.
paragraphs: Founded in 1989, this fireworks festival is entirely funded and organized by local residents and businesses in Osaka. It is held on the first Saturday of August each year (with some years adjusted to autumn due to construction).

Typically featuring over 10,000 fireworks, it is one of the most iconic large-scale fireworks displays in the Kansai region. The most famous feature is the 'Waterborne Continuous Rapid-Fire Fireworks,' which explode in a semi-circular pattern over the river, painting the night sky gold in sync with modern music.

There are many nearby stations, but they become extremely crowded. The northern bank of the Yodogawa can be accessed via Hankyu Kobe Line's 'Jūsō Station' or 'Tsukaguchi Station'; the southern bank near Umeda can be accessed via JR 'Osaka Station' or Hanshin 'Umeda Station.' Strict controls will be implemented on surrounding bridges starting from the afternoon of the event.

039.subtitle: Kyushu's only national-level fireworks competition, a fall sanctuary for Japan's top pyrotechnicians.
paragraphs: This is the largest and only 'National Fireworks Competition' in Kyushu, held annually on the third Saturday of October at the Kumagawa River Green Space in Yatsushiro City, Kumamoto Prefecture. With a total of 14,000 fireworks launched, it attracts hundreds of thousands of visitors from all over Japan.

Dozens of top fireworks artisans from across Japan will compete here, showcasing their skills and artistry with '5-inch' and 'shaku-dama' fireworks, as well as music-synchronized displays. A special commemorative firework, several hundred meters wide, will also be launched by the organizers.

Access is facilitated by the Shinkansen and local trains. Travelers can take the JR Kyushu Shinkansen or Higo-Orenji Railway to 'Shin-Yatsushiro Station' or 'Yatsushiro Station,' and then follow the crowd for about 10 to 15 minutes on foot to reach the viewing area along the Kumagawa River embankment.

040.subtitle: A spectacular autumn event lighting up the urban riverside, jointly held across the river with Setagaya Ward.
paragraphs: This large-scale urban fireworks festival is organized by Kawasaki City and takes place in mid-October each year. It features a joint display with the 'Setagaya Tamagawa Fireworks Festival' on the opposite bank in Setagaya Ward, with a total of 12,000 fireworks launched across the river.

Transitioning to an autumn event has successfully avoided the summer heat. The festival is characterized by precision rapid-fire fireworks synchronized with modern music, and the brilliant colors reflected on the wide surface of the Tamagawa River attract hundreds of thousands of residents from both banks each year.

Access is extremely convenient. On the Kawasaki side (Kanagawa), visitors mainly use the Tokyu Den-en-toshi Line, getting off at 'Futakoshinchi Station' or 'Kawatsu Station' and walking for 15 minutes; on the Setagaya side (Tokyo), 'Futakotamagawa Station' is the main access point. There will be extensive traffic control measures in place at both stations that evening.

041.subtitle: A metropolitan classic featuring spectacular large-scale fireworks against the backdrop of the Rainbow Bridge and Harumi Wharf.
paragraphs: This is one of Tokyo's flagship fireworks festivals, showcasing a strong sense of urban technology. The launch site is located on the surface of Tokyo Bay, in front of Harumi Wharf, with a total of 12,000 fireworks launched. Its biggest feature is the ability to capture fireworks alongside the Rainbow Bridge and the night view of Tokyo Bay.

This festival is one of the few events in central Tokyo that can launch 'shaku-go-sun-dama' (fireworks with an explosion diameter of up to 400 meters). With the redevelopment of the surrounding Harumi Passenger Terminal and the Olympic Village area, there have been significant adjustments to the viewing areas and paid seating in recent years.

Access varies by area. The main routes utilize the Toei Oedo Line from either 'Kachidoki Station' or 'Tsukiji Market Station', although some visitors choose to view the fireworks from the Odaiba or Toyosu sides. Due to the lack of buildings obstructing the view, many open areas along Tokyo Bay provide free vantage points to capture the spectacle.

042.subtitle: One of Japan's three major fireworks competitions, renowned for the autumn 'Tsuchiura Fireworks Special Launch'.
paragraphs: Founded in 1925 (Taisho 14), it is the pinnacle competition among 'Japan's three major fireworks'. It is held on the first Saturday of November at the Sakura Riverbank in Tsuchiura City, Ibaraki Prefecture, featuring up to 20,000 fireworks.

This is the ultimate battleground for fireworks artisans from all over Japan competing for the 'Prime Minister's Award'. The event is most famous for the 'Tsuchiura Fireworks Special Launch (Star Mine)' showcased by the organizers in the latter half, where thousands of fireworks explode in a synchronized display set to music, lighting up the night sky in a short span.

You can access the event by taking the JR Joban Line to 'Tsuchiura Station'. On the day of the festival, over 700,000 people flood into this small town of only 100,000 residents, turning the roads from the station to the riverbank (about a 30-minute walk) into a sea of people, and the shuttle bus wait times are usually quite long.

043.subtitle: A winter festival fireworks display that ranks among Japan's three great festivals and UNESCO World Heritage, performed alongside magnificent floats in the cold winter night.
paragraphs: This is a winter fireworks display that combines over 300 years of history and is registered as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage under the 'Chichibu Night Festival.' It takes place every year on the evening of December 3rd, launched around Chichibu Park (Otabisho) in Chichibu City, Saitama Prefecture.

On that evening, several tons of exquisitely decorated 'Yatai' and 'Kasaboko' floats will ascend steep slopes towards the Otabisho. At this time, rare giant aerial fireworks and golden weeping willows will simultaneously light up the winter night sky, creating a stunning visual of traditional festival and fireworks in perfect harmony.

Transportation can be accessed via the Seibu Railway Chichibu Line Limited Express to 'Seibu-Chichibu Station' or by taking the Chichibu Railway to 'Chichibu Station.' As one of the few places in Japan that launches thousands of fireworks in December, the local temperature is extremely low that night, so attendees should prepare complete winter clothing.

051.subtitle: A dazzling display of a thousand fireworks at the southern tip of Osaka Bay, creating a dreamlike experience by the seaside.
paragraphs: The Izumi Dream Fireworks Festival takes place on the Izumi Coast in Izumisano City, Osaka Prefecture, themed around 'dreams.' It features over a thousand meticulously designed fireworks and is a signature summer event on the southern side of Osaka Bay, held annually from mid to late August.

With a focus on the 'Starry Night' performance, the fireworks showcase large bowl-shaped fireworks, complemented by the reflective effect of the open sea of Osaka Bay, creating a dual visual impact. The venue is lively and warm, featuring yukata stalls and a food market.

The nearest station is Nankai Electric Railway's 'Izumisano Station,' which is about a 20-minute walk to the seaside venue, or you can enjoy the view from the surrounding streets. It is recommended to leave at least an hour in advance, as some roads will be subject to traffic control that evening.

052.subtitle: Autumn nights at Kobe Port, where fireworks bloom over the Seto Inland Sea embraced by Mount Rokko.
paragraphs: Kobe Port Minato HANABI is an annual maritime fireworks festival held in the Kobe harbor area every autumn. The main venue is Meriken Park, where fireworks are launched from offshore platforms, creating a stunning three-dimensional view framed by the Rokko mountain range.

The event spans multiple nights, with each show lasting about 30 minutes and featuring over 5,000 fireworks in total. The cool autumn sea breeze provides a comfortable viewing experience, and the relaxed atmosphere, distinct from the large summer fireworks displays, is its greatest charm.

You can reach Kobe Port by walking or taking the Port Liner; it’s about a 15-minute walk from Sannomiya Station to Meriken Park. Since the festival runs for several consecutive days, you can choose the best day to enjoy the show.

053.subtitle: A charming summer night at Ashiya Beach, kicking off the Kansai summer vacation with fireworks.
paragraphs: The Ashiya Summer Carnival is an annual summer celebration held in late July at Ashiya Beach Park in Ashiya City, Hyogo Prefecture. It combines a market, music performances, and fireworks, creating a vibrant summer night festival that showcases local culture for residents and visitors alike.

The fireworks display lasts about 20 to 30 minutes. While it may not be as grand as large-scale competitions, the peninsula topography of Ashiya Beach perfectly complements the fireworks with the sea level of Osaka Bay, creating an exquisite and romantic atmosphere that is loved by couples and families.

The nearest stations are Hanshin Electric Railway's Ashiya Station or Otaru Station, both about a 15 to 20-minute walk to the beach park. It is recommended to arrive early in the evening to enjoy the sunset and the full summer experience of the fireworks.

054.subtitle: A symphony of fireworks lighting up the Bon Festival night sky along the clear riverbanks of Osaka and Hyogo.
paragraphs: The Inagawa Fireworks Festival takes place along the banks of the Inagawa River, which flows through the border of Osaka and Hyogo Prefectures. It is a beloved summer fireworks event for locals in the Ikeda and Itami areas, held annually during the Bon Festival.

With approximately 5,000 fireworks launched, the festival atmosphere is enhanced by the Bon Festival celebrations, filling the riverbank with food stalls and crowds in yukata, preserving the warmth of traditional Japanese summer festivals. The fireworks reflect on the clear river surface, showcasing a tranquil beauty that contrasts with larger firework displays.

The nearest stations are Hankyu Electric Railway's Ikeda Station or Ishibashi-Hanadaimae Station, both about a 10 to 15-minute walk to the venue. Due to the large crowds during the Bon Festival, it is advisable to avoid the last train times and return early.
